THE LIMITS OF FREE EXPRESSION: A DEFENCE OF REGULATED SPEECH IN DEMOCRATIC SOCIETIES

In March 2021, the social media platform Parler was removed from Apple's App Store and suspended by Amazon Web Services following the United States Capitol attack, provoking fierce debate over whether private corporations and governments possess the moral authority to restrict speech in the name of public safety. The question at the heart of this controversy is one that has troubled political philosophers since John Stuart Mill published On Liberty in 1859: can a democratic society ever justifiably restrict freedom of expression? This essay argues that freedom of speech, while foundational to democratic life, is not an absolute right. Drawing on Mill's harm principle, contemporary scholarship on hate speech and epistemic injustice, and the concept of proportionality, I contend that carefully delimited speech restrictions—those that prevent tangible harm without suppressing dissenting viewpoints—are not only justifiable but necessary for the preservation of democratic discourse itself.

Any meaningful discussion of speech restrictions must begin with a clear definition of the terms at stake. Freedom of speech, as operationalized in this essay, refers to the legal and social guarantee that individuals may express ideas, opinions, and information without state censorship or retaliation. This definition excludes actions that use speech as a vehicle for conduct already criminalized by law, such as direct threats of violence or the incitement of imminent lawless action (Brandenburg v. Ohio, 1969). The concept of restriction, meanwhile, encompasses both formal legal prohibitions and the less visible but equally consequential mechanisms of social sanction, such as de-platforming and institutional speech codes. Crucially, a distinction must also be drawn between censorship—the pre-publication suppression of ideas based on their content—and regulation, which targets the harmful consequences of speech rather than the ideas themselves. This essay is concerned exclusively with the latter.

The most influential philosophical justification for restricting speech derives from John Stuart Mill's harm principle, articulated in On Liberty. Mill contends that "the only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any member of a civilized community against his will is to prevent harm to others" (Mill 68). This principle establishes a powerful framework: speech that causes demonstrable injury to identifiable individuals or groups may, in principle, warrant restriction. Modern scholars have extended Mill's logic to address contemporary concerns. Jeremy Waldron, in The Harm in Hate Speech, argues that hate speech constitutes a form of "group defamation" that systematically undermines the dignity and equal standing of targeted minorities within the public sphere (Waldron 47). When a Muslim student is told that her religion makes her inherently violent, or when a gay teenager encounters slogans declaring his identity a moral aberration, the harm inflicted is not merely psychological but civic: the targeted individual is effectively told that the democratic community does not recognize her as a full and equal participant. Empirical research supports this theoretical framework. A longitudinal study by Soral, Bilewicz, and Winiewski found that repeated exposure to discriminatory hate speech normalizes prejudice and desensitizes bystanders to the suffering of targeted groups, thereby eroding the very norms of mutual respect upon which democratic deliberation depends (Soral et al. 137). Under the harm principle, speech that inflicts this kind of civic injury meets the threshold for justified restriction.

Nevertheless, the case against any restriction of speech is formidable and must be confronted directly. The most compelling argument in favour of near-absolute free expression flows from what political theorists call the "marketplace of ideas" model, first articulated by Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes in his famous dissent in Abrams v. United States (1919): "the best test of truth is the power of the thought to get itself accepted in the competition of the market." The reasoning is twofold. First, epistemological humility demands that no orthodoxy—however widely accepted—is immune to challenge. Copernicus, Darwin, and Galileo were all denounced in their time, and the freedom to dissent from prevailing wisdom is the engine of intellectual progress (Mill 87). Second, and perhaps more subtly, the very act of suppressing speech risks granting it a credibility it does not deserve. When a society bans certain ideas, it creates martyrs and conspiracy theories; when it permits even offensive views to be aired and refuted, it demonstrates confidence in the resilience of its own normative foundations. Critics of hate speech legislation, such as Nadine Strossen in HATE: Why We Should Resist It with Free Speech, Not Censorship, further warn that speech restrictions are inevitably vulnerable to "mission creep" (Strossen 203). Once the state acquires the power to suppress speech deemed harmful, there is no principled mechanism to prevent that power from being deployed against dissenting political speech—a concern that finds empirical support in the record of authoritarian regimes that routinely invoke "national security" or "social harmony" to silence opposition.

Acknowledging the power of both positions, the most intellectually honest resolution lies not in choosing a side but in synthesizing their respective insights. The marketplace of ideas is not a self-regulating machine; it is a human institution that can fail when structural inequalities prevent certain voices from participating on equal terms. As Miranda Fricker demonstrates in Epistemic Injustice: Power and the Ethics of Knowing, when a speaker's social identity causes her testimony to be systematically discredited—a phenomenon Fricker calls "testimonial injustice"—the marketplace is not functioning as a neutral arbiter of truth but as a mechanism for reproducing existing hierarchies (Fricker 32). Hate speech exacerbates this dynamic by reinforcing the prejudicial stereotypes that produce testimonial injustice in the first place. To borrow an analogy from economics: just as a free market requires antitrust regulation to prevent monopolies from stifling competition, a free marketplace of ideas requires speech regulation to prevent the discursive monopolization of public space by powerful majorities. The appropriate standard for such regulation is proportionality. As George Fletcher explains in his analysis of constitutional balancing, proportionality requires that any restriction on a fundamental right must serve a legitimate aim, be rationally connected to that aim, be no more intrusive than necessary, and be proportionate in the narrow sense—that is, the benefits must outweigh the costs to the right in question (Fletcher 112). A law prohibiting racial epithets directed at individuals in public spaces arguably satisfies all four criteria; a law prohibiting criticism of government policy does not.

In conclusion, the claim that freedom of speech may never be justifiably restricted rests on an oversimplified conception of both speech and democracy. Speech is not merely the expression of ideas; it is also a form of social action capable of inflicting tangible harm on the dignity, safety, and civic standing of individuals and groups. When such harm is demonstrable, disproportionate, and unamenable to counter-speech, restriction is justified not in spite of democratic values but in their service. The challenge for democratic societies is not whether to restrict speech but how to do so with the precision, humility, and self-correction necessary to preserve the openness that makes democracy possible. The harm principle, fortified by the concept of proportionality and attentive to the reality of epistemic injustice, offers the most philosophically defensible framework for navigating this tension.

Works Cited

Brandenburg v. Ohio. 395 U.S. 444. Supreme Court of the United States, 1969.

Fletcher, George P. "The Theory of Proportionality." Harvard Law Review, vol. 122, no. 5, 2009, pp. 108-132.

Fricker, Miranda. Epistemic Injustice: Power and the Ethics of Knowing. Oxford University Press, 2007.

Mill, John Stuart. On Liberty. 1859. Edited by Elizabeth Rapaport, Hackett Publishing, 1978.

Soral, Wiktor, et al. "Exposure to Hate Speech Increases Prejudice through Desensitization." Aggressive Behavior, vol. 44, no. 2, 2018, pp. 136-146.

Strossen, Nadine. HATE: Why We Should Resist It with Free Speech, Not Censorship. Oxford University Press, 2018.

Waldron, Jeremy. The Harm in Hate Speech. Harvard University Press, 2012.
